text: And a single stab wound was used in the left subcostal area for retraction of the liver.
Here's the initial view.
The colon is somewhat distended and partially obstructing the view.
Patient had a free, a fair amount of free chylous ascites within the abdomen, but this did not affect the procedure.
The right lobe of the liver is elevated, showing the kidney, and then the left hand operating port is placed.
This is a three millimeter reusable port.
and then again a small stab wound for a retractor is placed in this bright subcostal margin.
The kidney is visualized and the retroperitoneal space is opened using the 3mm sealer.
A combination of blunt and sealer dissection is used to
opened the retroperitoneum and then drove his fascia to gain access to the kidney.
The hepatic flexure of the colon is taken down somewhat to provide better exposure.
This technique of sealing and then gently separating the tissues works quite well for avascular planes as well as areas where there is just one to two millimeter vessels.
The colon is further mobilized, better exposing the hilum of the kidney.
The liver retractor is primarily a three millimeter sucker, which can also be used to clear fluid out of the area.
Occasionally, a trans-abdominal stitch can be used for the same thing, but in this case, the extra access site allows for suction as needed.
The retroperitoneal fat is slowly dissected off the kidney
and the hilum is exposed.
You can see it is safe to use the sealer in proximity of both the liver and the bowel.
As there is no electric current spreading from outside of the device, there is no risk of injury to surrounding structure.
all the thermal energy is directed between the tips of the device.
The lower pole is gradually mobilized, again using a combination of blunt and sealer dissection, and the adrenal gland is identified and preserved.
Here you can see the hilum becoming more evident
and the hylar vessels are dissected out to provide better access for use of the staple.
The ureter is coming down off inferiorly and will be taken separately.
With the hylar vessels exposed, the camera is switched to the
right hand port and the umbilical port is used for the 5mm stapler and a single application of the stapler is used to take all the vessels.
The stapler is applied, the tissues are compressed for 10 seconds and then fired and here you can see the staple line with complete hemostasis.
The ureter is now addressed.
The section of the lower pole is continued until it is completely free, better exposing the ureter.
And then the ureter is dissected out bluntly down to the pelvic brim.
This can actually be carried on all the way down to the bladder wall if necessary.
The ureter is then cut so that an endo loop can be applied.
This is a 3-0 PDS endo loop.
And again, this is applied through the umbilical trochlea site.
Second endo loop is applied just to ensure that the ligature is watertight.
The residual ureter is excised.
Mobilization of the kidney is then completed.
and the last attachments are taken.
As there is no concern for malignancy in this case, the kidney is divided in situ to allow for extraction through the smaller umbilical trocar site.
The kidney is simply divided sharply into
smaller pieces.
And these will then be placed into a specimen bag formed out of the thumb of a number eight glove.
This is inserted through the umbilical trocar side.
And then the pieces are placed in it for removal.
This entire procedure took 25 minutes.
Patient tolerated the procedure extremely well.
Was started on feeds immediately following surgery.
and had complete resolution of his renal hypertension post-operatively.
Here you see the specimens being placed in the finger of the glove.
This is then extracted through the umbilical trotar site.
